title: How to Pack a Storage Unit Like a Pro
excerpt: Maximize every square foot with these proven loading strategies — from heavy-on-the-bottom basics to leaving an aisle for the items you'll actually need.
content:
Whether you're storing for a few weeks during a move or planning a long-term setup for business inventory, how you pack the unit decides whether you'll fight your stuff or breeze in and out.

Start with a floor plan

Before the first box goes in, sketch the unit. Heaviest, sturdiest items along the back wall — appliances, dressers, sealed boxes of books. Lighter, fragile, frequently-accessed items toward the front.

Build vertical, not horizontal

A 10x10 unit is also 8 feet tall — that's 800 cubic feet, not 100 square feet. Use uniform-size boxes so they stack flat. Wardrobe boxes go in last with hanging clothes still on hangers.

Leave a center aisle

The single most common mistake we see: customers pack a wall of stuff and then can't reach the holiday decorations behind it. A 24-inch aisle down the middle pays for itself the first time you need something.

Climate-control the right items

If you're storing electronics, leather, wood furniture, photos, or anything with adhesive, pay for a climate-controlled unit. Kansas summers are not kind to anything with a circuit board.
author: Stormont Self Storage Team

title: Climate-Controlled vs. Standard Storage: Which Do You Actually Need?
excerpt: Climate control is worth it for some belongings and a waste of money for others. Here's how to tell the difference before you sign a lease.
content:
Climate-controlled units typically run $20–$60 more per month than standard drive-up units. For some items that's the smartest insurance you'll buy all year. For others it's pure overpay.

Always climate-controlled

  • Wood furniture and antiques — humidity warps and cracks
  • Electronics, audio equipment, computers
  • Leather goods and upholstered pieces
  • Photos, artwork, important documents
  • Musical instruments
  • Pharmaceuticals or anything moisture-sensitive

Standard is fine

  • Lawn equipment and tools
  • Plastic bins of seasonal decor
  • Most automotive parts
  • Construction materials

The Overland Park climate factor

Our region swings from 100°F summers with 70%+ humidity to single-digit winters. A standard unit will mirror those swings within a few degrees. If you'd be uncomfortable leaving it in your garage for a year, climate control is the call.
author: Stormont Self Storage Team

title: The 30-Day Move Checklist (With a Storage Unit in the Plan)
excerpt: A week-by-week timeline for using self-storage to make a residential move dramatically less stressful.
content:
Most moves are stressful because everything happens in the same 48 hours. A short-term storage unit decouples the timeline so you can pack ahead, stage out-of-season items, and only handle each box once.

30 days out: reserve the unit

Online reservations lock the rate and hold the size. We recommend reserving one size larger than you think — overflow is cheaper than a second trip.

21 days out: pack the non-essentials

Holiday decor, off-season clothing, books, tools you don't use weekly, decor and art. Move it to the unit as you pack, not on move day.

14 days out: stage furniture you're keeping but not using

Guest-room furniture, the second couch, the bedroom set you're saving for the kid heading to college. Storage now means less truck space later.

7 days out: switch utilities, confirm movers

By now your unit should be 60–70% full and your house should already feel emptier.

Move day: only essentials and big furniture

The truck handles the daily-use stuff. Anything decorative, seasonal, or sentimental is already at the storage unit waiting.

2 weeks after: reassess

Most customers downsize their unit (or close it out) within 30 days of move-in. Don't overpay because you forgot to revisit.
author: Stormont Self Storage Team

title: Why Small Businesses Are Replacing Warehouse Leases with Self-Storage
excerpt: E-commerce sellers, contractors, and event vendors are using 10x20 and 10x30 storage units as low-overhead inventory headquarters.
content:
Commercial warehouse space in the Kansas City metro starts around $1.10 per sq ft per month, with a five-year lease minimum. A 10x30 climate-controlled storage unit gives you 300 sq ft for under $300/month with month-to-month terms and 24/7 access.

Who this works for

  • Amazon FBA sellers staging shipments
  • Etsy and online sellers holding inventory
  • Trade contractors storing tools and materials between jobs
  • Event vendors with seasonal equipment
  • Retailers consolidating overflow stock

What you give up

You can't run public-facing operations from a storage unit — no signage, no walk-in customers, no employees clocking in. If you need a storefront, this isn't the answer.

What you keep

Climate control, gated access, 24/7 entry on most facilities, drive-up loading on ground-floor units, and the ability to grow or shrink your footprint without renegotiating a lease. We see businesses run from a single 10x30 for years before they outgrow it.
author: Stormont Self Storage Team

title: Storing an RV, Boat, or Second Vehicle: What to Know Before You Park
excerpt: Outdoor parking, covered canopy, and fully enclosed units each make sense for different vehicles and budgets. Here's how to decide.
content:
Most HOAs and city ordinances around Overland Park don't allow RVs, boats, or commercial vehicles to be parked at a residence long-term. Storage facilities give you three tiers of vehicle storage at very different price points.

Open outdoor parking — most affordable

A paved or gravel pad inside the gated perimeter. Best for boats on trailers, work trucks, and second vehicles. Expect to pay $50–$120/month depending on length.

Covered canopy — middle option

A roof keeps off direct sun and most precipitation. The biggest win is UV protection — fiberglass, vinyl decals, and tires last dramatically longer. Expect $90–$180/month.

Enclosed unit — premium

A 10x30 or 12x35 drive-up unit gives full enclosure, climate control on some sites, and the ability to work on the vehicle inside. Best for classic cars, motorcycles, and high-value RVs. Expect $200–$450/month.

Prep before storing

  • Fill the fuel tank and add stabilizer for any storage over 30 days
  • Disconnect the battery or attach a maintainer
  • Inflate tires above normal — they will lose pressure
  • Cover or pull wiper blades off the windshield
author: Stormont Self Storage Team
